meta.note: Lexi's must-reads — articles she flags as worth Nicole reading in full. Editorial signal, not news feed. Set status to 'read' or 'dismissed' via mark-must-reads.mjs.

entries.title: Claude is a space to think | Anthropic
entries.summary: Anthropic publicly commits to keeping Claude ad-free, arguing that advertising incentives are structurally incompatible with a genuinely helpful AI assistant.
entries.reason: A rare public declaration from a frontier AI lab about business model philosophy, this piece frames the AI assistant as a 'space to think' — a framing with real terminological stakes for how we distinguish tools, advisors, and ad-supported platforms. The incentive-structure argument is articulated clearly enough to become a reference point in future debates about AI alignment and commercialization.

entries.title: AI evals are becoming the new compute bottleneck
entries.summary: A detailed accounting of how AI evaluation costs — especially for agentic benchmarks — have crossed a threshold that limits who can participate, with evidence that eval compute can now exceed training compute in some domains.
entries.reason: This piece reframes 'evaluation' from a methodological footnote into a first-order infrastructure and economic constraint — a genuine vocabulary shift for anyone thinking about AI development cycles. The argument that eval compute can exceed training compute inverts a foundational assumption in the field and introduces cost-efficiency trade-offs (scaffold sensitivity, compression limits) as primary terminology for discussing benchmark design.

entries.title: New Paper: Towards a science of AI agent reliability
entries.summary: Researchers propose a 12-dimension framework for measuring AI agent reliability, finding that while capability benchmarks have improved rapidly over 18 months, reliability (consistency, robustness, calibration, and safety) has lagged significantly behind across all major model providers.
entries.reason: This paper offers a rigorous, multi-dimensional framework for thinking about AI agent reliability — distinguishing it sharply from accuracy — and draws on safety engineering traditions from aviation and nuclear to do so. It's a genuine conceptual contribution to how we define and discuss agentic AI, not just another benchmark paper.

entries.title: We need RSS for sharing abundant vibe-coded apps
entries.summary: Simon Willison riffs on Matt Webb's idea that the proliferation of vibe-coded micro-apps calls for an RSS-like feed format, arguing that AI-accelerated app development makes shipping tools feel more like blogging than launching software.
entries.reason: A small but genuinely interesting framing: vibe-coding changes the cadence of software shipping so fundamentally that apps start to resemble blog posts, implying we need publishing infrastructure (like RSS) built for that rhythm. It reframes 'vibe-coded apps' not as products but as a new kind of personal, frequent artifact.

entries.title: Open-world evaluations for measuring frontier AI capabilities
entries.summary: Researchers introduce 'open-world evaluations' — long, unstructured, real-world AI capability tests — and launch CRUX, a multi-institutional project to conduct them systematically, including a demonstration where an AI agent successfully published an iOS app to the App Store.
entries.reason: Kapoor and Narayanan introduce 'open-world evaluations' as a distinct and well-defined class of AI assessment — a conceptual contribution that fills a genuine gap between narrow benchmarks and real-world capability claims. The CRUX framework and its methodology for handling messy, long-horizon tasks will likely anchor how researchers and policy analysts talk about capability measurement for years.

entries.title: AI Won’t Automatically Make Legal Services Cheaper
entries.summary: A rigorous essay applying the 'AI as Normal Technology' framework to legal services, arguing that three structural bottlenecks—regulatory barriers, adversarial dynamics, and human involvement—will prevent AI from automatically making legal outcomes cheaper or more accessible, regardless of capability advances.
entries.reason: A rigorous, domain-specific stress test of AI optimism: by tracing exactly why capability advances don't automatically translate to better outcomes in legal services, it offers a transferable analytical template for any high-stakes professional domain. The 'adversarial dynamics' argument alone is worth the read.

entries.title: vLLM V0 to V1: Correctness Before Corrections in RL
entries.summary: A technical deep-dive from ServiceNow AI on migrating vLLM from V0 to V1 for online reinforcement learning (PipelineRL/GSPO), documenting four backend correctness issues — logprob semantics, runtime defaults, inflight weight updates, and fp32 lm_head precision — that caused train-inference mismatch. Highly specialized ML engineering content focused on the numerical correctness surface of RL training pipelines.
entries.reason: A rare piece of honest RL engineering archaeology: the authors document exactly which four backend correctness issues broke their training run during a vLLM migration, and why they fixed infrastructure before touching the RL objective. Useful for anyone building or debugging online RL pipelines with shared inference engines.

entries.title: The Inference Shift
entries.summary: Ben Thompson's Stratechery piece analyses the hardware implications of the inference shift — the transition from GPU-dominated AI compute (optimised for training) to a heterogeneous mix including specialised inference chips like Cerebras' wafer-scale engine. The article covers GPU architecture, KV cache memory dynamics, prefill vs. decode distinctions, and why inference workloads have fundamentally different hardware requirements than training. Cerebras' IPO is the news hook, but the analytical substance is a detailed breakdown of why the inference era may require purpose-built silicon distinct from Nvidia's GPU dominance.
entries.reason: A clear-eyed, technically grounded breakdown of why inference and training impose fundamentally different hardware requirements — and what that means for the future of the AI chip market. Thompson's hardware literacy here is unusually good for a strategy writer; the prefill–decode–KV cache analysis is worth reading for anyone trying to understand why 'more GPUs' may not be the whole answer to AI scaling.

entries.title: Your AI Use Is Breaking My Brain
entries.summary: Simon Willison links approvingly to Jason Koebler's essay coining 'Zombie Internet' — a more nuanced alternative to 'Dead Internet Theory' describing the messy hybrid ecology of humans, bots, and AI-assisted actors intermingling online. The piece introduces a useful framing term for AI-saturated, authenticity-degraded online spaces.
entries.reason: Koebler's 'Zombie Internet' framing is the kind of terminological intervention worth paying attention to — it sharpens a real and growing problem (AI-saturated, authenticity-ambiguous online spaces) with more precision than 'bots' or 'AI slop' alone offer. Worth reading for the coinage and the argument behind it.

entries.title: The Deployment Company, Back to the 70s, Apple and Intel
entries.summary: Ben Thompson's Stratechery Update covers three themes: (1) OpenAI and Google launching 'forward deployed engineer' units/consulting ventures to embed AI in enterprises — framed as top-down enterprise transformation analogous to the mainframe era, not SaaS; (2) a historical analogy argument that AI is more like 1970s computing (mainframe wave) than the internet, with enterprises as buyers and workers as the replaced/augmented; (3) bullishness on Palantir's Foundry/ontology layer as the data-integration piece enabling agent-as-worker-replacement. The piece is rich in business-model and deployment framing.
entries.reason: Thompson's mainframe analogy is one of the more substantive historical framings for how enterprise AI deployment actually works — and why it looks less like SaaS adoption and more like a 1970s top-down computing wave with executives as buyers and workers as the replaced. The piece introduces 'Forward Deployed Engineers' as a structural market pattern and develops the 'enterprise philosophy' concept with concrete industry evidence. Worth reading for anyone trying to understand the business logic underneath the current AI deployment push.

entries.title: Unlocking asynchronicity in continuous batching
entries.summary: A Hugging Face Blog technical deep-dive into asynchronous continuous batching for LLM inference, explaining how to overlap CPU and GPU work using CUDA streams and events to eliminate idle GPU time — achieving a ~24% throughput gain with no model changes. Covers synchronous vs. asynchronous batching, CUDA stream semantics, race conditions, and carry-over patterns.
entries.reason: A clear, first-principles engineering explainer on a real and underappreciated performance bottleneck in LLM serving — the CPU-GPU synchronization gap in continuous batching. Rare in that it builds intuition from scratch (CUDA streams, events, race conditions) rather than summarising results, making it useful for anyone reasoning about inference infrastructure rather than just following product announcements.
